Guideline: Use gin.New() with explicit middleware in production
Description: gin.Default() includes Logger and Recovery middleware which may not suit production needs. Use gin.New() and add only the middleware you need for better control.
Do: Use gin.New() and attach middleware explicitly
Dont: Use gin.Default() blindly in production
Code Good:
r := gin.New()
r.Use(gin.Logger())
r.Use(gin.Recovery())
Code Bad: r := gin.Default() // includes Logger + Recovery you may not want

Guideline: Use r.Group() for API versioning and logical grouping
Description: Group related routes under a common prefix to keep your router organized and enable per-group middleware such as auth or rate limiting.
Do: Group routes by version or feature domain
Dont: Define all routes flat on the root router
Code Bad:
r.GET("/api/v1/users", listUsers)
r.POST("/api/v1/users", createUser)
r.GET("/api/v2/users", listUsersV2)

Guideline: Write middleware as functions returning gin.HandlerFunc
Description: Middleware should return gin.HandlerFunc so it can accept configuration parameters. Call c.Next() to continue the chain or c.Abort() to stop it.
Do: Return gin.HandlerFunc from a factory function
Dont: Put middleware logic directly inline in route registration

Guideline: Validate JWT tokens in dedicated middleware
Description: Extract and validate the JWT from the Authorization header in middleware. Set the parsed claims into the context so downstream handlers can access the authenticated user.
Do: Parse token in middleware and set claims via c.Set()
Dont: Validate tokens inside each individual handler

Guideline: Use structured logging middleware for request tracing
Description: Log request method, path, status code, latency, and client IP in a structured format. Use a logger like zerolog or zap instead of the default gin.Logger for production.
Do: Use structured logging with latency and status tracking
Dont: Rely solely on gin.Logger() with its default text format
Code Bad:
r := gin.Default() // uses default text logger
// no structured fields, hard to parse in log aggregators

Guideline: Configure CORS with explicit allowed origins
Description: Use the gin-contrib/cors package with specific origins, methods, and headers. Never use AllowAllOrigins in production as it exposes your API to any domain.
Do: Specify exact allowed origins and methods
Dont: Use AllowAllOrigins: true or set Access-Control-Allow-Origin to * manually
Code Good:
import "github.com/gin-contrib/cors"

r.Use(cors.New(cors.Config{
    AllowOrigins:     []string{"https://example.com"},
    AllowMethods:     []string{"GET", "POST", "PUT", "DELETE"},
    AllowHeaders:     []string{"Origin", "Authorization", "Content-Type"},
    ExposeHeaders:    []string{"Content-Length"},
    AllowCredentials: true,
    MaxAge:           12 * time.Hour,
}))

Guideline: Use custom recovery middleware with error reporting
Description: The default gin.Recovery() writes to stderr. In production wrap it with custom logic to send panics to an error tracking service like Sentry.
Do: Use gin.CustomRecovery to report panics to monitoring
Dont: Let panics crash silently or only go to stderr
Code Bad:
r.Use(gin.Recovery())
// panics only go to stderr, no alerting

Guideline: Use ShouldBindJSON instead of BindJSON
Description: ShouldBindJSON returns an error without automatically writing a 400 response, giving you control over the error format. BindJSON calls c.AbortWithError internally.
Do: Use ShouldBindJSON and handle the error yourself
Dont: Use BindJSON which auto-aborts with a default 400 response

Guideline: Use binding tags for declarative input validation
Description: Gin uses go-playground/validator under the hood. Add binding tags to struct fields for required fields, string length, email format, and more.
Do: Declare validation rules in struct tags
Dont: Write manual validation logic in every handler

Guideline: Register custom validators with the binding engine
Description: For business rules not covered by built-in validators, register custom validation functions using the validator engine obtained from binding.Validator.
Do: Register custom validators at startup with the validator engine
Dont: Duplicate custom validation logic across handlers

Guideline: Return consistent error responses with gin.H
Description: Always return errors as JSON with a consistent structure including an error message and optional details. Use appropriate HTTP status codes.
Do: Return structured JSON errors with proper status codes
Dont: Return plain text errors or inconsistent error shapes

Guideline: Wrap all responses in a standard JSON envelope
Description: Define a response struct with success, data, and error fields. This makes it easier for frontend clients to parse responses uniformly.
Do: Use a response helper that wraps data in a consistent envelope
Dont: Return raw data or different shapes from different endpoints

Guideline: Use c.FormFile for single uploads and c.MultipartForm for multiple
Description: Limit file size with r.MaxMultipartMemory. Validate the file type and size before saving. Use c.SaveUploadedFile for convenience.
Do: Validate file type and size before saving; set MaxMultipartMemory
Dont: Accept any file without validation or size limits

Guideline: Use r.Static and r.StaticFS for serving static assets
Description: Serve static files from a dedicated directory. Use r.StaticFile for individual files and r.Static for directories. Never serve the entire project root.
Do: Serve a specific directory with r.Static()
Dont: Serve the project root or use a broad path
Code Good:
r.Static("/assets", "./public/assets")
r.StaticFile("/favicon.ico", "./public/favicon.ico")
Code Bad: r.Static("/", ".") // exposes entire project directory including .env, go.mod, etc.

Guideline: Use r.LoadHTMLGlob or LoadHTMLFiles for template rendering
Description: Load templates at startup and render them with c.HTML. Pass data using gin.H. Use template functions for formatting within templates.
Do: Load templates once at startup with LoadHTMLGlob
Dont: Parse templates on every request

Guideline: Use c.Param for path params and c.Query for query params
Description: Distinguish between path parameters (/users/:id) and query parameters (/users?page=1). Always validate and convert parameter types before use.
Do: Validate and convert params with proper error handling
Dont: Use raw string params without validation or conversion

Guideline: Set trusted proxies explicitly with SetTrustedProxies
Description: When behind a reverse proxy, configure trusted proxies so that c.ClientIP() returns the correct client IP from X-Forwarded-For. Pass nil to trust no proxies.
Do: Call SetTrustedProxies with your actual proxy IPs or nil
Dont: Leave the default which trusts all proxies

Guideline: Use http.Server with signal handling for graceful shutdown
Description: Do not use r.Run(). Create an http.Server and use Shutdown() with a context to drain in-flight requests before stopping. Listen for OS signals to trigger shutdown.
Do: Use http.Server and os.Signal for graceful shutdown
Dont: Use r.Run() which does not support graceful shutdown
Code Bad:
r := gin.Default()
r.GET("/", handler)
r.Run(":8080") // no graceful shutdown, connections dropped on SIGTERM

Guideline: Implement rate limiting as middleware using a token bucket
Description: Use golang.org/x/time/rate or a distributed limiter backed by Redis. Apply rate limiting per client IP or per API key to prevent abuse.
Do: Use a per-IP or per-key rate limiter middleware
Dont: Apply a single global limiter or skip rate limiting entirely

Guideline: Use context timeout middleware to prevent long-running requests
Description: Wrap each request context with a deadline so that slow database queries or external calls are cancelled after the timeout period.
Do: Set a per-request timeout using context.WithTimeout
Dont: Let requests run indefinitely with no timeout

Guideline: Parse page and limit from query params with sensible defaults
Description: Always validate and cap pagination parameters. Set reasonable defaults and maximum limits to prevent clients from requesting too much data.
Do: Validate and cap pagination params with defaults and max limits
Dont: Trust client-supplied pagination values without bounds

Guideline: Prefer stateless token-based auth over server-side sessions
Description: For APIs use JWT or opaque tokens stored in the Authorization header. Sessions require server-side state and sticky sessions which complicate horizontal scaling.
Do: Use token-based auth for APIs with the Authorization header
Dont: Store auth state in cookies with server-side sessions for APIs

Guideline: Use the repository pattern to decouple database access from handlers
Description: Inject a repository interface into handlers or use gin context to pass the database connection. This makes handlers testable and database-agnostic.
Do: Inject repository interfaces and access them via context or struct
Dont: Use a global DB variable directly in handlers

Guideline: Use httptest.NewRecorder with the gin router for handler tests
Description: Create the gin router in test mode, register routes, and use httptest to send requests and assert responses. Set gin.SetMode(gin.TestMode) to suppress debug output.
Do: Use httptest.NewRecorder and gin.SetMode(gin.TestMode)
Dont: Test by starting a real HTTP server or skip testing handlers
Code Good:
func TestGetUser(t *testing.T) {
    gin.SetMode(gin.TestMode)
    r := gin.New()
    r.GET("/users/:id", handler.GetUser)

    w := httptest.NewRecorder()
    req, _ := http.NewRequest("GET", "/users/1", nil)
    r.ServeHTTP(w, req)

    assert.Equal(t, 200, w.Code)
    var resp map[string]interface{}
    json.Unmarshal(w.Body.Bytes(), &resp)
    assert.NotNil(t, resp["data"])
}

Guideline: Use swaggo/gin-swagger for auto-generated API docs
Description: Annotate handlers with swaggo comments and use gin-swagger middleware to serve the Swagger UI. Run swag init to generate the docs from annotations.
Do: Use swaggo annotations and generate docs at build time
Dont: Write OpenAPI specs manually and keep them separate from code

Guideline: Upgrade HTTP to WebSocket using gorilla/websocket with gin
Description: Use the gorilla/websocket Upgrader in a gin handler to upgrade the connection. Handle read/write in a goroutine loop and check the origin in the Upgrader.
Do: Use gorilla/websocket Upgrader with origin checking
Dont: Implement WebSocket from scratch or skip origin validation

Guideline: Use c.Stream or c.SSEvent for server-sent events
Description: Gin supports streaming via c.Stream and SSE via c.SSEvent. Set proper headers for SSE and flush after each event. Use the client-gone channel to stop streaming.
Do: Use c.Stream with proper SSE headers and flush
Dont: Buffer the entire response before sending or ignore client disconnect

Guideline: Use r.Handle for non-standard HTTP methods
Description: Gin supports custom HTTP methods via r.Handle. Use this for WebDAV methods like PROPFIND or custom methods your API requires.
Do: Use r.Handle() for non-standard methods
Dont: Ignore non-standard methods or hack around routing

Guideline: Register a custom NoRoute handler for 404 responses
Description: Use r.NoRoute to return a proper JSON 404 response instead of the default HTML page. Also use r.NoMethod for 405 responses.
Do: Set r.NoRoute and r.NoMethod with JSON responses
Dont: Let gin return the default HTML 404 page for an API

Guideline: Set gin.ReleaseMode, trusted proxies, and server timeouts for production
Description: In production set GIN_MODE=release or call gin.SetMode. Configure read/write timeouts on http.Server and set trusted proxies. Disable debug logging.
Do: Configure release mode and http.Server timeouts for production
Dont: Run gin.Default() with default settings in production
Code Good:
gin.SetMode(gin.ReleaseMode)
r := gin.New()
r.Use(gin.Recovery())
r.SetTrustedProxies([]string{"10.0.0.0/8"})

srv := &http.Server{
    Addr:         ":8080",
    Handler:      r,
    ReadTimeout:  5 * time.Second,
    WriteTimeout: 10 * time.Second,
    IdleTimeout:  120 * time.Second,
}
srv.ListenAndServe()
